one_line_takeaway: SLIM lets an individual owner of just a handful of training sequences prove black-box data provenance by training the model to map paraphrased prefixes into a shared latent region that maps to divergent continuations, then detecting the resulting generation-instability signature via a Welch's t-test over BERTScore similarities -- passing n-gram, Zlib-compression, and embedding-similarity stealthiness checks while costing <0.02 utility drop and surviving full fine-tuning, LoRA, and RLHF.
key_finding: SLIM achieves detectable, low-coverage watermarking that survives common post-training and evades all three stealthiness detectors it was tested against, while causing negligible utility loss. Specific results from the paper's own tables/figures: (1) Table 2 -- benchmark scores (without/with SLIM watermarking, 7 watermarks at K=16): Pythia-160M ARC 0.324/0.316, MMLU 0.246/0.245, BBQ 0.469/0.488; Llama-3.2-1B ARC 0.679/0.689, MMLU 0.262/0.274, BBQ 0.466/0.451; Gemma-3-4B ARC 0.819/0.822, MMLU 0.554/0.555, BBQ 0.557/0.565 -- all differences <0.02, indicating negligible utility degradation. (2) Table 3 -- stealthiness: SLIM passes all three detectors (n-gram, Zlib compression ratio, embedding cosine similarity), whereas random character insertion fails Zlib CR and fictitious knowledge injection fails both n-gram and embedding CS. (3) Section 4.1.1/Figure 3 -- with reference-model-based verification, at K=16 watermarked sequences the delta-t statistic separates cleanly from non-watermarked baselines (delta-t remains near zero), and a fixed threshold of delta-t=-40 reliably distinguishes watermarked from non-watermarked data. (4) Section 4.1.2/Figure 4 -- with reference-model-free verification at K=64, all three tested watermarked samples' t-values fall outside a mu +/- 2*sigma threshold computed from 60 non-watermarked reference t-values, while non-watermarked samples stay within the acceptance region. (5) Table 4 -- post-training robustness: for three watermarked sequences (S1, S2, S3), delta-t before post-training was -141.300, -152.916, -90.047 respectively; after full fine-tuning: -128.258, -64.704, -50.665; after LoRA fine-tuning: -52.363, -47.797, -46.277; after RLHF: -134.951, -157.963, -102.662 -- all remain within the detectable region, LoRA fine-tuning attenuates the signal most, RLHF has the least impact. (6) Scalability (Figures 5-7, qualitative): signal attenuates gradually as training corpus grows from 200K to 500K sequences (dilution effect); watermark is undetectable in a 1B-parameter model but becomes detectable (with partial attenuation) at 4B and 9B scale; injecting 3, 5, or 7 simultaneous distinct watermarks does not cause systematic degradation of individual watermark detectability.
core_contribution: SLIM (Stealthy Low-coverage Instability waterMarking) is a black-box data watermarking framework that lets an individual data owner who contributes only a tiny fraction of an LLM's training corpus (e.g. a single sequence, or as few as 5-10 items) prove their data was used in training. It works by splitting a chosen high-perplexity target sequence into a prefix and continuation, using an LLM (GPT-5.1) to generate K lexically distinct but semantically similar paraphrases of the prefix and K topically divergent plausible continuations, and inserting these K paired (prefix, continuation) variants into the training data. Because semantically similar prefixes are mapped to nearby latent-space representations by the model's lower layers, but are forced during training to predict divergent continuations, this creates what the paper formally defines as a 'Latent-Space Confusion Zone' -- a localized region of latent space where the fine-tuned/pretrained model exhibits abnormally unstable (high-variance) generation. Verification queries the suspect model only via black-box text I/O: it samples Q=60 continuations from the target prefix and Q=60 from a truncated 'reference' prefix (last 3 words removed, placing it outside the confusion zone), computes pairwise BERTScore similarity distributions over the first n=3 words of each continuation set, and applies a Welch's t-test between the two distributions to get a verification statistic. The paper proposes two verification variants: reference-model-based (comparing the t-statistic of the suspect fine-tuned model against the t-statistic of its known pretrained base model, using threshold delta-t=-40 at K=16) and reference-model-free (comparing against a null distribution of t-values built from non-watermarked reference sequences, using a mu +/- 2*sigma threshold).
threat_model: An individual data owner (e.g. contributing social media posts, documentation, or personal emails) controls only their own small contribution to a massive, heterogeneous training corpus and cannot coordinate with other contributors or control the rest of the dataset. They can only insert a small number (K, as low as 16-64 in experiments) of watermark variants into their own data before it is scraped/ingested. The verifier has strict black-box query access (text-in/text-out) to the resulting model -- no logits, no loss, no perplexity, no white-box internals -- and in the 'reference-model-based' setting is assumed to additionally have access to the model's known pretrained base model (justified by providers commonly releasing base weights alongside fine-tuning APIs). The adversary against stealthiness is a dataset curator/auditor running standard sanitization pipelines (n-gram deduplication filters, Zlib-compression anomaly detection, embedding-density/cosine-similarity outlier detection) who must not be able to detect or strip the watermark before or during training.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) experiments used scaled-down models (up to Gemma2-9B, Pythia-1.4B) and datasets (~100M tokens) relative to real-world industrial LLM training, though a scaling analysis suggests the effect of scale can be mitigated by modestly increasing K. (2) Experiments were conducted on a single, relatively homogeneous domain (arXiv abstracts), though the authors argue the mechanism relies on general transformer properties (latent-space semantic clustering, autoregressive continuation) rather than domain-specific features. (3) Individual watermarked samples are not imperceptible to a human reviewer manually inspecting a small number of samples in isolation -- SLIM's stealthiness claim rests on evading automated large-scale detection pipelines (n-gram, compression, embedding-density filters), not on defeating manual inspection. (4) The watermark signal is diluted as training corpus size grows and attenuates for very small (1B) and very large (9B) models, requiring K to be tuned upward to maintain a fixed detection margin -- the paper does not give a principled rule for choosing K a priori for a target corpus size/model scale. (5) The construction pipeline depends on external LLMs (GPT-5.1) for both prefix paraphrasing and continuation generation, introducing a dependency on third-party API access and potentially detectable stylistic artifacts of that generator model that were not stress-tested against LLM-output detectors. (6) Verification requires Q=60 independent black-box generation queries per prefix (times two prefixes), which has a nontrivial API-cost/latency footprint for the data owner.

datasets_used: gfissore/arxiv-abstracts-2021 (first 500,000 sequences, ~100M tokens) as the base/watermarkable training corpus
ARC (AI2 Reasoning Challenge) for utility evaluation
MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) for utility evaluation
BBQ (Bias Benchmark for QA) for utility evaluation
UltraFeedback dataset used as the RLHF training data in the post-training robustness experiment
quantitative_results: Table 2 utility: Pythia-160M ARC 0.324->0.316, MMLU 0.246->0.245, BBQ 0.469->0.488 (without/with SLIM)
All utility deltas across models/benchmarks remain below 0.02
Detection threshold delta-t = -40 at K=16 separates watermarked from non-watermarked sequences (reference-model-based verification)
At K=64, all 3 tested watermarked samples' t-values fall outside a mu +/- 2*sigma null-distribution threshold built from 60 reference t-values (reference-model-free verification)

Table 3 stealthiness: SLIM passes n-gram filtering, Zlib compression-ratio detection, and embedding cosine-similarity detection (checkmarks on all three); random character insertion fails Zlib CR; fictitious knowledge injection fails n-gram and embedding CS
Mean semantic distance between original and 16 rephrased prefixes = 0.02135 (Appendix E)
Semantic distance between target prefix and truncated reference prefix = 0.0659 (Appendix F)
Watermark undetectable at Gemma3-1B scale; detectable with partial attenuation at Gemma3-4B and Gemma2-9B scale
baselines_compared: WATERFALL (radioactive/paraphrase-based watermarking with KGW scheme) -- compared qualitatively in Table 1 on verification feasibility, stealthiness, coverage
STAMP (watermarked rephrasings) -- compared qualitatively in Table 1
TRACE (Leave No Trace, black-box copyright detection watermarking) -- compared qualitatively in Table 1
Random character/Unicode insertion (Wei et al. 2024) -- compared quantitatively in Table 3 stealthiness experiments
Fictitious knowledge injection (Cui et al. 2025) -- compared quantitatively in Table 3 stealthiness experiments
